The project is a new research facility on York University Campus with 2-storeys in building height. The Project will be connected to the existing Sherman Health Sciences Facility by walkways at Levels 1 and 2. Level 1 (basement) contain labs with restricted access that will be classified as a Group F, Division 3 major occupancy. Level 2 (first storey) will include consultation offices and meeting spaces. Level 3 will consist of offices and workstations. Levels 2 and 3 will be classified as Group D major occupancies.

The subject site has been developed as a single-storey commercial plaza comprised of four buildings with a total gross floor area of 4,877 square metres. The buildings have been recently completed and will be occupied by a mix of retail, restaurant and service commercial uses. The proposed Official Plan Amendment would redesignate the easterly portion of the subject lands from Core Employment Areas to General Employment Areas and would introduce a Site and Area Specific Policy (SASP) applicable to the subject site. The SASP would permit retail and service commercial uses across the entirety of the site, which is currently split designated as Core Employment Areas and General Employment Areas by the City of Toronto Official Plan. The SASP would also prohibit major retail developments of 6,000 square metres or more of gross floor area.

Proposed 11-storey mixed-use building at 847-855 Kingston Road with 99 residential units. It would have a total gross floor area of approximately 7,195 square metres, including 6,823 square metres of residential space and 371 square metres of retail commercial space at grade. 32 vehicle parking spaces, 109 bicycle parking spaces and 1 Type G loading space is proposed. This file was originally circulated and commented on as a 7-storey proposal containing 29 residential units, ground floor commercial, and 2 levels of below grade parking at 847-853 Kingston Rd. The property at 855 Kingston Rd has since been purchased, and the proposal has been updated to reflect the new lot consolidation.

The application to amend the Zoning By-law proposes two new buildings for the property at 333-351 King Street East & 200 Front Street East. The proposed development includes a 37-storey residential building (140 metres tall) on the west side of the site and a 28-storey office building (127 metres tall) in the centre of the site. The footprint of the new office building would replace some of the existing retail uses fronting on Front Street East. The existing Globe and Mail Centre, Coca-Cola/George Brown building and existing buildings fronting on King Street East would remain in place. The proposal also includes two privately owned publicly-accessible spaces fronting onto Front Street East, and a child care facility with outdoor space on the second floor of the proposed office building.

Zoning By Law Amendment and concurrent Draft Plan of Subdvision applications to create 13 blocks within the William Baker Neighbourhood. Planned as new inclusive, compact and complete community that is walkable and well served by transit and active transportation. The Neighbourhood is intended to be developed over phases with a broad range and mix of uses, built forms, densities, housing and unit types and tenures (including affordable and seniors options), all supported by an existing higher order transit and existing and planned community services and facilities. The District Plan for the William Baker Neighbourhood includes development blocks at the south end proposed for a mix of high/mid and low-rise residential as well as mixed use blocks at the intersection of Keele Street and Sheppard Avenue, to create a Neighbourhood Focus at that gateway to the community featuring commercial uses and parks, in a location well served by transit. Building heights in this area are proposed to range from 4 to 20 storeys. Residential blocks proposed in the north portion, which will be developed through subsequent phases, provide for similar heights in the range of 4 to 20 storeys, with potential for some taller elements near the Downsview Park subway/GO station hub. Of great importance to the future of the Neighbourhood is preservation and enhancement of the woodlot. It will be a central feature of the new community and further enhanced with additional parkland being added around its edges as contemplated by the Secondary Plan. Additionally, a park at the southern end of the Neighbourhood is proposed to feature a new pedestrian/cycling bridge that will provide a convenient link to Downsview Park to the south.
